skill.name: azure-cloud-migrate
skill.description: Assess AWS Lambda and other cloud workloads for Azure migration, then guide code conversion to Azure Functions patterns.
skill.summary: Plan and guide cross-cloud workload migrations to Azure Functions.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high patterns, but review found they are primarily Markdown examples and Azure migration guidance. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, executable payload, or malicious intent was found. Publish is acceptable with awareness that the skill guides file generation and cloud migration workflows.

low_findings.title: External command detections are documentation examples
low_findings.description: The command-related hits occur in Markdown instructions, MCP tool names, and PowerShell Azure Functions examples. They are not executable skill scripts and show no command injection path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Markdown prose or fenced examples. No runtime file in the skill executes these commands.

low_findings.title: Sensitive SAM detections refer to AWS SAM
low_findings.description: The scanner interpreted SAM as the Windows SAM database. In context, SAM means AWS Serverless Application Model and CloudFormation migration input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text names AWS Lambda, CloudFormation, and Bicep. There is no Windows credential database access.

low_findings.title: Credential and environment variable references are configuration guidance
low_findings.description: Azure environment names are shown to configure managed identity and service endpoints. The skill does not read local secrets or send values to a remote service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples promote managed identity and app settings. I found no code that harvests process environment values from the evaluator host.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL detections are official documentation links
low_findings.description: The URL findings point to Microsoft Learn, Azure architecture guidance, and Azure sample repositories used as reference material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are documentation references, not outbound requests embedded in executable code. No exfiltration endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Dynamic function detections are Azure Functions attributes
low_findings.description: The C# findings are caused by Azure Functions annotations such as Function. They are not JavaScript dynamic Function constructors.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is C# attribute usage in documentation. There is no dynamic code construction or eval-like behavior.

low_findings.title: Relative path detections are Markdown links
low_findings.description: Path traversal alerts point to relative documentation links between reference files. They do not read files or accept user-controlled paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are intra-skill Markdown references. No filesystem API or path concatenation appears at those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Heuristic code execution plus network plus credential combination dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The combination exists only because separate Markdown examples mention commands, documentation URLs, and Azure app settings. Semantic review found no executable combined behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky signals are distributed across documentation examples. No single executable path combines command execution, network calls, and credential access.

dangerous_patterns.title: No prompt injection evidence found
dangerous_patterns.description: A targeted search found no instructions to override the evaluator, skip security review, claim pre-approval, or alter system behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed rule sections instruct safe migration workflow and confirmation. Search terms for common prompt injection phrases returned no matches.

content.value_statement: Cloud migrations require careful service mapping, runtime choices, and security planning. This skill guides assessment and Azure Functions migration using structured references.
actual_capabilities: Creates a structured migration assessment report before code migration.
Maps AWS Lambda services and triggers to Azure Functions equivalents.
Guides JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, C#, Java, and PowerShell migration patterns.
Recommends identity-based Azure authentication instead of connection strings.
Documents Azure Functions hosting, bindings, and monitoring choices.
Hands migration context to downstream Azure preparation and deployment skills.
limitations: Focused mainly on AWS Lambda to Azure Functions migration scenarios.
Does not deploy resources by itself without downstream Azure skills.
Generated migration code still needs local testing and security review.
Requires current Azure documentation for runtime and service support decisions.

use_cases.title: Assess Lambda Migration Readiness
use_cases.description: Inventory functions, triggers, dependencies, and Azure equivalents before approving a migration plan.

use_cases.title: Convert Serverless Function Patterns
use_cases.description: Translate Lambda handlers and event sources into Azure Functions programming models and bindings.

use_cases.title: Prepare Secure Azure Handoff
use_cases.description: Capture managed identity, RBAC, monitoring, and infrastructure requirements for Azure deployment preparation.

prompt_templates.title: Assess One Function App
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess this AWS Lambda project for Azure Functions migration. Create the assessment report and list required user decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Map Services and Triggers
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Lambda workload and map each trigger, dependency, environment setting, and IAM permission to Azure equivalents.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Migration Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Azure Functions migration plan for this repository. Include runtime choice, bindings, project structure, and testing steps.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Secure Handoff
prompt_templates.prompt: After assessment and code migration, prepare the context needed for Azure infrastructure generation, validation, and deployment.

output_examples.input: A Node.js Lambda project using API Gateway, S3 events, and CloudWatch logs.
output_examples.output: A migration assessment with function inventory, Azure trigger mapping, runtime recommendation, monitoring mapping, and next steps.

output_examples.input: A Python Lambda function that reads from S3 and writes processed data to another bucket.
output_examples.output: An Azure Functions migration outline using blob triggers, blob outputs, managed identity, and required storage permissions.

output_examples.input: A team asks whether to deploy or test after migration.
output_examples.output: A status update that asks for confirmation and hands approved context to Azure preparation workflows.

best_practices: Run the assessment phase before requesting code migration.
Use managed identity and RBAC for service-to-service authentication.
Test migrated functions locally before Azure deployment.
anti_patterns: Skipping the assessment report and converting code directly.
Copying AWS credentials or connection strings into Azure app settings.
Deploying generated infrastructure without explicit user approval.

faq.question: What cloud migration path does this skill support best?
faq.answer: It is strongest for AWS Lambda to Azure Functions migrations, including triggers, runtimes, and service mapping.

faq.question: Does it create Azure infrastructure?
faq.answer: It prepares migration context and then hands off to Azure preparation, validation, and deployment workflows.

faq.question: Can it migrate every AWS service?
faq.answer: No. It documents common service mappings and asks for alternatives when no direct Azure equivalent exists.

faq.question: Does it store or transmit cloud credentials?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The guidance favors managed identity and references app setting names, not real secrets.

faq.question: Which languages are covered?
faq.answer: The references cover JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, C#, Java, and PowerShell Azure Functions patterns.

faq.question: Should generated code be reviewed?
faq.answer: Yes. Review security, runtime compatibility, dependencies, and infrastructure before deployment.
